enrichment.short_description: Flat-file, database-free CMS and template engine for building fast, version-controllable websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Automad is an open-source, flat-file content management system and template engine that runs without a database. All pages and settings are stored as plain, human-readable text files, which makes an entire site easy to back up, diff, and put under version control with Git.

It ships with a browser-based dashboard that includes a block-style page editor and an in-page editing mode, so content can be adjusted directly while browsing the live site. A built-in caching layer and headless JSON API mode round out the toolset for developers who want to use Automad as a backend for a separate frontend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Automad suits developers and freelancers building small-to-medium marketing sites, portfolios, or blogs who want a lightweight, database-free alternative to WordPress and prefer keeping content in files they can track with Git.

features.title: Flat-file storage
features.description: Pages and content live in plain text files rather than a database, simplifying backups and version control.

features.title: Block editor
features.description: Browser-based editor for composing pages from reusable content blocks.

features.title: In-page editing
features.description: Edit content directly on the live page while browsing the site as an admin.

features.title: Headless JSON API
features.description: Optional read-only API mode for using Automad as a backend to a separate frontend.

features.title: PHP template engine
features.description: Custom template language for building themes without a full framework.

features.title: Built-in caching
features.description: Multi-layer caching designed to keep flat-file sites fast under load.

pros: No database to install, back up, or maintain
Content in plain files works cleanly with Git-based workflows
Free and open source under the MIT license
Lightweight footprint suitable for shared hosting
cons: Flat-file storage scales less predictably than a database for very large sites
Smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than mainstream CMS platforms like WordPress
Relies on community/volunteer maintenance rather than a company with formal support contracts

faqs.question: Is Automad free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Automad is released under the MIT license and is free to download, self-host, and modify.

faqs.question: Does Automad require a database?
faqs.answer: No. Automad stores all content in flat text files, so no database setup is required.

faqs.question: Can Automad be used headless?
faqs.answer: Yes, Automad offers a read-only JSON API mode for use as a headless content backend.

faqs.question: What do I need to host Automad?
faqs.answer: A standard PHP-capable web server is sufficient since there is no database dependency.

use_cases.title: Version-controlled marketing sites
use_cases.description: Teams that want their entire site content stored as files they can track in Git alongside code.

use_cases.title: Lightweight blogs and portfolios
use_cases.description: Individuals who want a simple CMS without the overhead of installing and maintaining a database.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Automad is a free, open-source flat-file CMS and template engine that stores content in plain text files with no database required.

enrichment.short_description: Simple, database-free flat-file CMS that stores content as JSON files for fast, self-hosted blogs and sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Bludit is an open-source, flat-file content management system that stores posts and pages as JSON text files instead of relying on a database. It positions itself around simplicity, speed, and security, and only requires a PHP-capable web server to run.

The platform supports both Markdown and HTML editing, ships with an official theme and plugin ecosystem, and includes built-in SEO fields. Bludit emphasizes privacy by default, with no visitor tracking baked into the core software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Bludit fits bloggers, small businesses, and developers who want a fast, no-database CMS that's simple to install on shared hosting and easy to back up by copying files.

features.title: Flat-file JSON storage
features.description: Content is stored as JSON files, eliminating the need for a database server.

features.title: Markdown and HTML editors
features.description: Write content in Markdown or a rich HTML editor, depending on preference.

features.title: Themes and plugins
features.description: Official repository of themes and plugins for extending site functionality.

features.title: Built-in SEO tools
features.description: On-page SEO fields for titles, descriptions, and friendly URLs.

features.title: Privacy-first design
features.description: No default visitor tracking, aimed at simplifying GDPR-friendly deployments.

pros: No database required, simplifying setup and backups
Fast page loads due to lightweight flat-file architecture
Free and open source under the MIT license
Simple admin interface aimed at non-technical editors
cons: Flat-file architecture can become harder to manage at very large content volumes
Smaller ecosystem of themes/plugins compared to database-driven CMS platforms
Community-maintained project without an enterprise support contract

faqs.question: Does Bludit need a database?
faqs.answer: No. Bludit stores all content as JSON files on disk, so no database installation is needed.

faqs.question: Is Bludit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bludit is open source under the MIT license and free to self-host.

faqs.question: What does Bludit require to run?
faqs.answer: A web server with PHP support is all that's needed; no database server is required.

use_cases.title: Fast personal or business blogs
use_cases.description: Sites that want quick load times and simple file-based backups without database overhead.

use_cases.title: Low-maintenance self-hosted sites
use_cases.description: Developers deploying lightweight sites on shared or low-resource hosting.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bludit is a free, open-source flat-file CMS that stores content as JSON files, requiring no database for fast, simple self-hosted sites.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP CMS built on Symfony and Twig with built-in REST and GraphQL APIs for headless use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Bolt CMS is an open-source content management system built on the Symfony framework and Twig templating. Current versions ship with both RESTful and GraphQL APIs out of the box, making it usable as a traditional CMS or as a headless content backend.

The editing experience includes Article and Redactor content editors, field-level internationalization, dummy content generation for development, and a default Tailwind CSS-based theme. Because it's built on Symfony, Bolt is compatible with the broader Symfony bundle ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Bolt CMS is aimed at developers already comfortable with the Symfony/PHP ecosystem who want a CMS foundation they can extend with standard Symfony components, or who need a headless-capable CMS with a REST/GraphQL API.

features.title: Symfony-based architecture
features.description: Built on Symfony components, allowing use of the Symfony bundle ecosystem for extensions.

features.title: REST and GraphQL APIs
features.description: Built-in APIs enable headless CMS usage alongside traditional page rendering.

features.title: Twig templating
features.description: Uses the Twig template engine common across Symfony projects for theme development.

features.title: Field-level translation
features.description: Multi-language support with translation control at the individual field level.

features.title: Multiple content editors
features.description: Includes both an Article editor and a Redactor rich-text editor for content creation.

features.title: Extensions marketplace
features.description: Directory of community and commercial extensions and developer services.

pros: Native REST and GraphQL APIs for headless or hybrid setups
Built on Symfony, giving access to a large, mature PHP ecosystem
Free and open source
Structured field-level translation support for multilingual sites
cons: Requires familiarity with Symfony conventions, raising the learning curve for non-Symfony developers
Smaller community and plugin ecosystem than larger CMS platforms like Drupal or Joomla
Release cadence has slowed compared to the project's earlier years

faqs.question: Is Bolt CMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Bolt CMS is open source and free to download and self-host; commercial developer services are offered separately by community members.

faqs.question: Can Bolt CMS be used headless?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in RESTful and GraphQL APIs for headless or hybrid frontend setups.

faqs.question: What framework is Bolt CMS built on?
faqs.answer: Bolt CMS is built on Symfony and uses Twig for templating.

use_cases.title: Headless content backend
use_cases.description: Teams that want a PHP-based headless CMS with a ready-made GraphQL API for a decoupled frontend.
use_cases.audience: Developers building JAMstack or decoupled sites

use_cases.title: Symfony-based websites
use_cases.description: Agencies already working in the Symfony ecosystem who want a CMS layer that fits their existing stack.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bolt CMS is a free, open-source PHP content management system built on Symfony with built-in REST and GraphQL APIs for headless use.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source PHP/MySQL content management system using the Smarty template engine, maintained by a nonprofit foundation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CMS Made Simple is an open-source content management system that has been in active development since 2004. It's maintained by the CMS Made Simple Foundation, a registered non-profit organization in Canada, and its codebase moved to GitHub in early 2025 to broaden community collaboration.

It uses the Smarty templating engine for design flexibility, giving designers control over markup without needing deep PHP knowledge, while developers can extend the system with modules, plugins, and a documented API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CMS Made Simple suits small businesses, agencies, and long-time users who want a straightforward, self-hosted PHP CMS without the complexity of larger enterprise platforms.

features.title: Smarty templating
features.description: Design layer built on the Smarty template engine, separating markup from PHP logic.

features.title: Module and plugin system
features.description: Extensible architecture supporting third-party modules, plugins, and themes.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: Documented API for building custom modules and integrations.

features.title: Non-profit governance
features.description: Maintained by the CMS Made Simple Foundation, a registered Canadian non-profit.

pros: Long-running, mature project with a stable core (latest release series 2.2.x)
Free and open source
Smarty templating gives designers layout control without heavy PHP knowledge
Governed by a non-profit foundation rather than a single commercial vendor
cons: Smaller community and extension marketplace than Drupal, Joomla, or WordPress
Requires MySQL/PHP hosting rather than a database-free setup
UI conventions feel dated compared to newer CMS platforms

faqs.question: Is CMS Made Simple free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source and free to download and self-host; the project accepts donations to support the non-profit foundation.

faqs.question: Who maintains CMS Made Simple?
faqs.answer: The CMS Made Simple Foundation, a registered non-profit organization based in Canada.

faqs.question: What templating engine does it use?
faqs.answer: CMS Made Simple uses the Smarty PHP template engine for its design layer.

use_cases.title: Small business websites
use_cases.description: Businesses that want a stable, self-hosted CMS without a steep learning curve.

use_cases.title: Long-term site maintenance
use_cases.description: Organizations already on CMS Made Simple that want to continue with a maintained, community-driven platform.
use_cases.audience: Existing CMS Made Simple site owners

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CMS Made Simple is a free, open-source PHP/MySQL CMS using Smarty templating, maintained by a non-profit foundation since 2004.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, accessibility-focused CMS built on Symfony for professional websites and web applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Contao is an open-source content management system, originally released in 2006 as TYPOlight and renamed Contao in 2010. It's licensed under LGPL-3.0 and built on the Symfony framework, giving developers access to thousands of Symfony bundles for extending functionality.

Accessibility is a core design goal: Contao supports building sites that meet standards such as BITV 2.0 and Germany's BFSG accessibility law, and its own admin interface is largely accessible. The platform also supports multiple websites per installation, multilingual content, granular user permissions, HTTP/2, Varnish caching, and automated sitemap generation, alongside modern security features like passkey/2FA login.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Contao is popular with agencies and organizations in the DACH (Germany/Austria/Switzerland) region and with any team that needs to meet formal web accessibility requirements for public-sector or enterprise sites.

features.title: Accessibility compliance tooling
features.description: Built to support BITV 2.0 and BFSG accessibility standards, with an accessible admin interface.

features.title: Symfony foundation
features.description: Built on the Symfony framework, giving access to a large bundle ecosystem for customization.

features.title: Multi-site management
features.description: Run multiple websites from a single Contao installation.

features.title: Multilingual content
features.description: Native support for managing content across multiple languages.

features.title: Modern authentication
features.description: Supports passkey and two-factor authentication with automatic lockouts after failed logins.

features.title: Performance caching
features.description: Supports Varnish HTTP caching and HTTP/2 for high-traffic sites.

pros: Strong accessibility focus, useful for public-sector and compliance-driven sites
Free and open source under LGPL-3.0, usable in commercial projects
Built on Symfony, giving access to a mature bundle ecosystem
Established project with roots going back to 2006
cons: Community and documentation skew heavily toward the German-speaking market
Requires Symfony/PHP familiarity for deeper customization
Smaller international extension ecosystem than Drupal or Joomla

faqs.question: Is Contao free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Contao is licensed under LGPL-3.0 and free to use, including for commercial projects.

faqs.question: What was Contao previously called?
faqs.answer: Contao was originally released in 2006 as TYPOlight and renamed Contao in 2010.

faqs.question: Does Contao support accessibility standards?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's built to help sites meet accessibility requirements such as BITV 2.0 and Germany's BFSG law.

use_cases.title: Accessibility-compliant public sites
use_cases.description: Public sector or institutional sites that must meet formal accessibility regulations.

use_cases.title: Multi-site agency deployments
use_cases.description: Agencies managing multiple client websites from a single Contao installation.
use_cases.audience: Web agencies, especially in the DACH region

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Contao is a free, open-source, accessibility-focused CMS built on Symfony, used for professional websites since 2006 (formerly TYPOlight).

enrichment.short_description: Template-driven, open-source PHP CMS that converts static HTML/CSS templates into editable sites without coding.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CouchCMS is an open-source content management system aimed at web designers who want to turn existing static HTML/CSS templates into editable websites. Instead of writing PHP, designers wrap sections of their markup in special tags to mark them as editable, cloneable, or listable.

It's released under the Common Public Attribution License 1.0, which allows free commercial use with attribution; a paid commercial license is also available to remove branding and add a period of premium support. Core features include editable regions, cloned pages for blogs and portfolios, comment moderation, SEO-friendly nested URLs, form handling, an events calendar, site search, RSS feeds, and PayPal integration for selling digital goods.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: CouchCMS is built for front-end designers and freelancers who already have HTML/CSS templates and want to make them client-editable without learning a full PHP framework.

features.title: Editable regions
features.description: Mark sections of existing HTML templates as editable using simple tags.

features.title: Cloned pages
features.description: Generate blog posts, portfolio items, or event listings from a single template.

features.title: SEO-friendly URLs
features.description: Nestable virtual folders produce clean, readable URLs.

features.title: Comments and moderation
features.description: Built-in visitor commenting with moderation controls.

features.title: Forms and events
features.description: Self-validating forms and an events calendar with map integration.

features.title: PayPal integration
features.description: Sell digital goods directly through the CMS with automated payment handling.

pros: No PHP knowledge required to build a client-editable site from an HTML template
Free, open-source core under CPAL 1.0
Wide range of built-in features (forms, events, search, RSS) without extra plugins
Optional commercial license for white-labeling
cons: Smaller community and ecosystem compared to major CMS platforms
Template-tag approach can feel unfamiliar to developers used to standard PHP frameworks
Less frequent public updates than more actively marketed CMS projects

faqs.question: Is CouchCMS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core CMS is open source under the Common Public Attribution License 1.0; a paid commercial license is available for white-labeling.

faqs.question: Do I need to know PHP to use CouchCMS?
faqs.answer: No, CouchCMS is designed so front-end designers can make HTML/CSS templates editable using tags, without writing PHP.

faqs.question: Can CouchCMS sell digital products?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes built-in PayPal integration for selling digital goods.

use_cases.title: Converting static templates to CMS sites
use_cases.description: Designers who have a finished HTML/CSS template and want to make it editable for clients.

use_cases.title: Small business sites with blogs/events
use_cases.description: Sites needing blog posts, event listings, and forms without a heavy framework.

features: Editable regions, cloned pages, forms, events, search

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CouchCMS is an open-source, template-driven PHP CMS that turns static HTML/CSS templates into editable, client-friendly websites.

enrichment.short_description: Enterprise-grade open-source CMS with a composable, API-first architecture, maintained by the nonprofit Drupal Association.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Drupal is a large, open-source content management system originally created by Dries Buytaert in 2001 and now supported by the nonprofit Drupal Association along with a global community of contributors, certified partners, and thousands of experienced professionals.

It's distributed in two main forms: Drupal CMS, a curated distribution aimed at marketers and content teams, and Drupal Core, the underlying framework for developers building fully custom applications. Drupal's architecture is API-first and composable, supporting structured content, low-code/no-code options, and integrated AI tooling, and it's used to power hundreds of thousands of websites worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Drupal is best suited to organizations with complex content structures, multi-site needs, or enterprise governance requirements — from government agencies and universities to large media and corporate sites — where its flexibility and security track record justify a steeper setup curve than smaller CMS options.

features.title: Structured content modeling
features.description: Highly flexible content types and fields for modeling complex information architectures.

features.title: API-first / composable architecture
features.description: Headless and decoupled deployment options via Drupal's built-in APIs.

features.title: Granular permissions
features.description: Fine-grained role and permission system suited to large organizations and multi-editor teams.

features.title: Large module ecosystem
features.description: Thousands of contributed modules extending core functionality.

features.title: Enterprise security process
features.description: Coordinated security advisory and release process backed by the Drupal Security Team.

features.title: Drupal CMS distribution
features.description: A curated, easier-to-adopt starting point aimed at marketers and content editors.

pros: Free and open source with no vendor lock-in
Highly flexible for complex, structured, or multi-site content architectures
Backed by a large community, certified partner network, and a formal security process
API-first design supports headless and hybrid delivery
cons: Steeper learning curve than lighter CMS platforms, especially for non-developers
Can require more hosting resources and technical maintenance than simpler CMS options
Module quality and maintenance vary since the ecosystem is community-contributed

faqs.question: Is Drupal free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drupal is free, open-source software available as Drupal CMS (for content teams) or Drupal Core (for custom development).

faqs.question: Who maintains Drupal?
faqs.answer: Drupal is maintained by a global open-source community, supported by the nonprofit Drupal Association.

faqs.question: Can Drupal be used headless?
faqs.answer: Yes, Drupal has an API-first, composable architecture that supports headless and decoupled implementations.

faqs.question: When was Drupal created?
faqs.answer: Drupal was created by Dries Buytaert in 2001.

use_cases.title: Large institutional and government sites
use_cases.description: Organizations needing complex permissions, multi-site management, and a strong security process.
use_cases.audience: Government, higher education, and enterprise IT teams

use_cases.title: Headless/composable content backends
use_cases.description: Teams building decoupled frontends that consume Drupal via its APIs.
use_cases.audience: Development teams building custom digital experiences

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Drupal is a free, open-source enterprise CMS with a composable, API-first architecture, maintained by the nonprofit Drupal Association.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source electronic lab notebook for research teams to log experiments, manage inventory, and book equipment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: eLabFTW is a free, open-source electronic lab notebook (ELN) created by researcher Nicolas Carpi in 2012 to replace paper lab notebooks. It's released under the AGPLv3 license, with all features available with no paywall, and is maintained today by Deltablot, the company Carpi founded in 2019 to support the growing eLabFTW community.

Beyond experiment logging with timestamping and digital signatures, eLabFTW includes an inventory module for tracking lab resources such as plasmids, compounds, and antibodies, and a booking system for scheduling shared equipment with granular permissions. It supports export to PDF, CSV, JSON, and the ELN archival format, is available in 21+ languages, and aims for WCAG 2.1 accessibility compliance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: eLabFTW is built for academic and industry research labs that need a compliant, auditable digital record of experiments and shared resources, whether self-hosted on institutional infrastructure or run via Deltablot's managed hosting.

features.title: Electronic lab notebook
features.description: Log experiments and protocols with timestamping and digital signatures for auditability.

features.title: Inventory management
features.description: Track lab resources such as plasmids, compounds, and antibodies.

features.title: Equipment booking
features.description: Schedule shared equipment and resources with granular permission controls.

features.title: Multi-format export
features.description: Export data as PDF, CSV, JSON, or the ELN archival format.

features.title: Internationalization and accessibility
features.description: Available in 21+ languages and designed toward WCAG 2.1 accessibility compliance.

features.title: Security auditing
features.description: Regular code audits and a bug bounty program supporting the security posture of the platform.

pros: Fully free and open source under AGPLv3 with no feature paywall
Purpose-built for research compliance: timestamping, digital signatures, audit trails
Combines lab notebook, inventory, and equipment booking in one system
Managed hosting option available through Deltablot for teams that don't want to self-host
cons: Self-hosting requires Linux server administration expertise
Feature depth is focused on lab/research workflows, not general-purpose content management
Managed hosting is a paid service, separate from the free self-hosted software

faqs.question: Is eLabFTW free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted software is free and open source under AGPLv3 with all features included, no paywall.

faqs.question: Who develops eLabFTW?
faqs.answer: It was created by researcher Nicolas Carpi in 2012 and is maintained by Deltablot, the company he founded in 2019.

faqs.question: Can eLabFTW be hosted for me instead of self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, Deltablot offers managed/cloud hosting with PRO support as a paid alternative to self-hosting.

faqs.question: What can eLabFTW track besides experiments?
faqs.answer: It also includes inventory management for lab resources and a booking system for shared equipment.

use_cases.title: Academic research labs
use_cases.description: University labs replacing paper notebooks with a searchable, auditable digital record.

use_cases.title: Industry R&D teams
use_cases.description: Companies needing compliant experiment tracking and shared equipment scheduling.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: eLabFTW is a free, open-source electronic lab notebook for logging experiments, managing inventory, and booking equipment in research labs.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source CMS for building websites and web applications, built entirely by a volunteer community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Joomla! is a free, open-source content management system first released in 2005 and built entirely by a global volunteer community, governed by the nonprofit Open Source Matters, Inc. It's recognized as a Digital Public Good and reports over 155 million downloads and more than 2 million active websites.

The platform is search-engine and mobile friendly out of the box, with multilingual support, flexible design customization, a multi-user permission system, and an extensible architecture backed by more than 10,000 extensions and templates from its community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Joomla! suits organizations that need multi-user content workflows and multilingual sites out of the box — such as nonprofits, membership sites, and small-to-midsize businesses — without committing to a heavier enterprise platform like Drupal.

features.title: Multilingual support
features.description: Built-in tools for managing content across multiple languages without third-party plugins.

features.title: Multi-user permission levels
features.description: Granular access control for teams with multiple content editors and roles.

features.title: Extensions ecosystem
features.description: Over 10,000 community extensions and templates for adding functionality and design options.

features.title: SEO and mobile friendly defaults
features.description: Core designed to be search-engine friendly and responsive out of the box.

features.title: Flexible design system
features.description: Unlimited template-based design customization.

pros: Free and open source, built and maintained entirely by volunteers
Native multilingual support without requiring extra plugins
Large extension and template ecosystem
Established, long-running project (since 2005) with a large install base
cons: Smaller professional services/agency market than Drupal or WordPress in some regions
Admin interface has a steeper learning curve for first-time non-technical users
Relies on community-contributed extensions of varying maintenance quality

faqs.question: Is Joomla! free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Joomla! is free and open-source software governed by the nonprofit Open Source Matters, Inc.

faqs.question: Does Joomla! support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, multilingual content management is built into the core.

faqs.question: When was Joomla! first released?
faqs.answer: Joomla! was first released in 2005.

use_cases.title: Multilingual organizational websites
use_cases.description: Nonprofits, government bodies, or businesses needing native multi-language publishing.
use_cases.audience: Nonprofits, government sites, and multinational businesses

use_cases.title: Multi-editor content sites
use_cases.description: Teams needing structured, role-based publishing workflows.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Joomla! is a free, open-source CMS built by a volunteer community, offering multilingual support and a large extensions ecosystem.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source headless CMS built on Angular and Firebase for managing content, translations, and assets for global teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Localess is an open-source, headless content management system built with Angular and deployed on Firebase (Authentication, Firestore, Storage, and Functions) and Google Cloud infrastructure. It's released under the MIT license and described by its maintainers as free forever, with users only paying for the infrastructure they deploy it on.

The platform focuses on multilingual publishing: it centralizes translation workflows with assignment, review, and approval steps, offers AI-assisted translation through Google Translate integration, and provides visual schema tools so teams can define content fields, types, and relationships without engineering involvement. Content, translations, and assets are managed together, and a Google CDN integration is used to serve content quickly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Localess fits engineering-led teams already using Firebase/Google Cloud who need a lightweight, self-hosted headless CMS with strong multilingual and translation workflow support, without adopting a heavier enterprise CMS.

features.title: Headless content API
features.description: Content is managed centrally and delivered via API for use in any frontend.

features.title: Translation workflow management
features.description: Assign, review, and approve translations as part of the publishing process.

features.title: AI-assisted translation
features.description: Integrates with Google Translate to speed up translating content into multiple languages.

features.title: Visual schema builder
features.description: Define content fields, types, and relationships without writing code.

features.title: Asset management
features.description: Organize images, videos, and documents alongside content.

features.title: Firebase-native deployment
features.description: Runs on Firebase and Google Cloud, using Firestore, Auth, Storage, and Functions.

pros: Free and open source (MIT license); only pay for your own infrastructure
Purpose-built translation management with AI-assisted translation
Visual schema modeling accessible to non-engineers
Fast delivery via Google CDN integration
cons: Tied to the Firebase/Google Cloud stack, which limits hosting flexibility for non-GCP teams
Smaller, newer community compared to established headless CMS options
Requires familiarity with Firebase deployment concepts to self-host

faqs.question: Is Localess free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Localess is open source under the MIT license; you only pay for the Firebase/Google Cloud infrastructure you deploy it on.

faqs.question: What stack does Localess run on?
faqs.answer: It's built with Angular on the frontend and Firebase (Firestore, Auth, Storage, Functions) plus Google Cloud on the backend.

faqs.question: Does Localess help with translations?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes a translation management workflow with AI-assisted translation via Google Translate.

use_cases.title: Multilingual content operations
use_cases.description: Global teams that need structured translation review and approval workflows.
use_cases.audience: Localization and content teams at global companies

use_cases.title: Headless content for Firebase apps
use_cases.description: Engineering teams already on Firebase/Google Cloud who want a lightweight headless CMS.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Localess is a free, open-source headless CMS built on Angular and Firebase, with AI-assisted translation and content management for global teams.
